query: http caching cache-control etag 304 interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this browser, network, security, or state-management prompt, so broader JavaScript question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: MDN HTTP caching
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for Cache-Control, validators, freshness, revalidation, and browser caching behavior.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on freshness, validators, and 304 semantics.
theirGaps: It is broader than the interview prompt and less compressed into the deployment-safe strategy teams need for HTML, APIs, and immutable assets.

realWorldUseCases: Practical HTTP caching interview answer for frontend engineers: cache directives, validators, conditional requests, and deployment-safe caching strategy for HTML, APIs, and immutable static assets.
actionableExamples: Runnable example #1: cache headers by response class
followUpConfusion: Core mental model Use this sequence: (1) Is cached response fresh? If yes, serve locally. (2) If stale, send conditional request with validators. (3) Server returns 304 (reuse cached body) or 200 (replace cache entry).

realWorldUseCases: MDN covers caching thoroughly, but our page is more explicit about how to split strategy between HTML, APIs, and immutable static assets in production.
actionableExamples: The canonical docs still have strong examples and directive coverage, so the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is faster on the likely follow-up confusion: freshness versus validation, and why HTML and versioned assets should not share the same cache policy.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter HTML versus hashed-asset header example later if we need more example distance over MDN.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical caching reference, but our page is stronger for this interview question because it turns the spec into a deployment-safe policy split across HTML, APIs, and static assets.
